I do my best to provide the best evidence–based care to our patients, work with the highest standards of professionalism, and make sure I create an environment where my trainees, colleagues, and division staff enjoy working and learning.

I believe that respect, compassion, and a supportive work environment are at the core of successful clinical and academic practice. This is why I lead on staff wellbeing initiatives, offer a listening ear, and help to create a caring work environment by smiling, checking on people, and following up on matters of significance to others. As a DBT therapist, I am available for my patients 24–7 through phone coaching and often receive heartwarming remarks regarding their relationship with me, both verbal and written. When faced with challenges or complaints, such as billing complaints or disclosures about lack–of–fit in style with patients, I keep an open mind and help problem–solve by referring to appropriate colleagues and following up.

It is very important to use Epic in a smart and efficient way. Epic is a powerful system, but it only functions optimally if we input information intelligently—such as regularly updating the patient’s problem list, medications, and other key data. Unfortunately, this is often neglected, leading to inefficient and time–consuming visits and follow–ups. Most of the patients are long–term patients, yet their visits often take as much time as new ones because we need to fill in or update their Epic files, which were previously overlooked by others.

The real challenge in my practice is managing an overbooked schedule filled with extra patients, compounded by the high volume of messages that patients can easily generate thanks to the accessibility of our communication systems.

Because of my challenging job, many times the parents come anxious to my clinic. sometimes the parents do not accept the diagnosis of their children. Sometimes  the parents d not accept any small comment about their child . Sometimes the father and mother  come with conflicts between them which lowers their cooperation with the treating team and negatively affect their child (child neglect).
Also in one instance I gave an objective comment in a meeting which I discovered later to  negatively impact on one of my colleagues because of a personal issue related to that colleague.
With time I am learning to limit talking with patients and colleagues to the minimum necessary, to be objective most of the time, to talk about patients in closed rooms and to refer to psychiatry and child protection committee whenever the parents or children need it.
All this control of the situation can not be achieved without work life balance, something that I missed during the war time which caused me a lot of stress in clinical, research, teaching duties. From now on, I will be taking more vacations to decrease burn out. I also started daily physical exercise and writing down on a notebook the issues that bother me.
